name: Improve keyboard and precision interactions
description:
Improve keyboard and precision interactions where they materially speed up editing without adding complexity.

Context:
- This editor is about fast, focused edits.
- A small number of well-chosen precision interactions can improve flow significantly.

Candidate scope:
- keyboard shortcuts for undo, redo, export, or adding cuts
- marker or crop nudging
- snapping or precision aids where appropriate

Cross-cutting notes:
- Do not add broad complexity for marginal gains.
- Keep these behaviors integrated with the chosen undo/redo and command model.
- Favor the highest-value refinements only.

Acceptance criteria:
- The selected interactions improve speed and precision.
- The refinements fit naturally into the existing UX.
- Tests cover the most important behavior changes.

name: Tune performance and maintainability
description:
Investigate performance hotspots and simplify the implementation where needed while keeping the architecture aligned with project principles.

Context:
- The most likely hotspots are thumbnail extraction, working-video reloads, and timeline responsiveness.
- Performance work should be guided by observation and measurement, not premature complexity.

Scope:
- Identify slow or janky workflows.
- Measure enough to guide improvements.
- Simplify APIs or components that drift toward unnecessary complexity.
- Capture follow-up work for larger improvements in dex.

Cross-cutting notes:
- This is both a performance and maintainability task.
- The goal is not to build a complex optimization subsystem.
- Prefer the smallest changes that materially improve responsiveness or clarity.

Acceptance criteria:
- Major interaction bottlenecks have been investigated.
- Meaningful optimizations or simplifications have been made where justified.
- The implementation remains aligned with the project principles.
- Follow-up work is captured cleanly in dex if more is needed.

name: Build the minimal app shell with open, play, pause, seek, and reload
description:
Build the walking-skeleton application shell: open a video, play it with Qt multimedia, seek it, and reload a replacement working video path after edits.

Context:
- Basic playback itself is intentionally simple because Qt already solves most of it.
- The goal is not a custom playback engine; it is a clean shell for the editing loop.
- The key behavior is that after an edit is applied, the player can reload the new working video and continue the editing session.

Scope:
- App bootstrap.
- Simple QML screen.
- File-open flow.
- Qt multimedia integration for open/play/pause/seek.
- Backend bridge for current working video path.
- Reload behavior when the backend provides a replacement working video.

Cross-cutting notes:
- Keep the backend/QML boundary narrow and intentional.
- Do not embed complex editing orchestration into a giant main QML file.
- This slice should cooperate with the future session model and undo/redo direction.

Acceptance criteria:
- A user can open a video.
- A user can play, pause, and seek it.
- The player can reload a replacement working video cleanly.
- The boundary between QML and backend is minimal and understandable.
- Tests cover the core non-visual logic and at least one happy-path UI interaction.
result:
Built minimal app shell with video playback via PySide6/QML.

Components:
- app.py: Entry point, wires session → bridge → QML engine
- bridge.py: SessionBridge with hasVideo, workingVideoUrl props + openFile/close/setWorkingVideo slots
- qml/Main.qml: Video player, file dialog, play/pause, seek slider, time display

Key design decisions:
- Bridge is the ONLY Python↔QML interface (narrow boundary)
- QML reacts to videoChanged signal for reload behavior
- Session model stays pure Python, bridge adapts for QML

Verification:
- 29 tests passing (7 new bridge tests)
- App launches: uv run video-scissors
- Imports and QML file loading verified programmatically

Run: uv run video-scissors

commit.message: Add minimal app shell with video playback

name: Ship a basic timeline scrubber bound to the player
description:
Ship the first useful timeline scrubber bound to the player.

Context:
- The timeline is a signature area of the product, but the earliest slice should stay thin.
- This initial slice should make seeking and playhead feedback useful before adding thumbnail polish.

Scope:
- Add a timeline control in QML.
- Bind it bidirectionally to player position and media duration.
- Support seeking by drag and/or click.
- Show a clear playhead representation.

Cross-cutting notes:
- Keep the interaction simple and correct before visual polish.
- This slice should prepare for later thumbnail rendering and cut-marker interactions.
- Avoid leaking player details deep into unrelated backend logic.

Acceptance criteria:
- The playhead follows playback.
- User interaction on the timeline seeks the video.
- The feature is usable without thumbnails.
- Tests cover timeline math and at least one UI happy path.

name: Add focused end-to-end happy-path tests
description:
Add a small, high-value set of end-to-end happy-path tests across UI and backend.

Context:
- The project should respect the test pyramid, so end-to-end coverage should stay intentionally small.
- A few representative end-to-end flows will provide confidence that the major pieces work together.

Candidate flows:
- open video -> crop -> apply -> export
- open video -> add cut markers -> remove segment -> export
- open video -> perform edit -> undo -> redo

Cross-cutting notes:
- Prefer a few maintainable happy paths over a large brittle suite.
- Failures should produce screenshots or logs that support debugging.
- These tests should build on the screenshot/headless groundwork established earlier.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least two representative end-to-end flows exist.
- Failures produce useful screenshots or logs.
- The suite remains small, maintainable, and clearly worth its runtime cost.

name: Define the editor session model and media service boundaries
description:
Define the backend session model and media service boundaries that the rest of the editor will build on.

Context:
- The old project drifted toward a large backend object that mixed state management, FFmpeg orchestration, async work, and UI plumbing.
- This reboot should keep those responsibilities smaller and more explicit.
- The backend should model the editing session intentionally rather than accumulating file-path bookkeeping ad hoc.

Scope:
- Define source video versus current working video.
- Define the editor session model.
- Define media probe service interfaces.
- Define edit-application service interfaces.
- Define thumbnail service interfaces.
- Define export service interfaces.
- Establish clear names for edit requests, command inputs, and results.

Cross-cutting notes:
- FFmpeg should not be called directly from high-level application logic.
- The model should support crop, cut/remove, thumbnail generation, and export without turning into a god object.
- Business logic should remain testable without Qt where possible.
- The design should work with the chosen undo/redo strategy.

Acceptance criteria:
- The backend has explicit concepts for source and working video.
- High-level application logic depends on service interfaces, not FFmpeg calls.
- Tests cover the session model and service contracts.
- The design is small and clear enough to support later crop and cut slices without major reshaping.
result:
Defined editor session model and service protocol boundaries via TDD.

Session model (session.py):
- EditorSession: tracks source_video (immutable after load) and working_video
- load(), close(), set_working_video() for state transitions
- has_video property

Service protocols (services.py):
- MediaProbeService: probe() -> VideoInfo
- EditService: apply_crop(), apply_cut() -> EditResult
- ThumbnailService: generate() -> Path
- ExportService: export() -> Path

Data types (frozen dataclasses):
- VideoInfo (width, height, duration, codec)
- CropRequest (x, y, width, height)
- CutRequest (start, end)
- EditResult (output_path)
- ThumbnailRequest (timestamp, width, height)
- ExportRequest (destination)

Verification:
- 17 new tests (22 total) all passing
- Protocols define contracts without implementations
- Designed for undo/redo compatibility (immutable requests, explicit state)
- FFmpeg stays behind service boundaries

commit.message: Define editor session model and service protocols

name: Build Video Scissors
description:
# Plan: Build Video Scissors

## Summary
Build a rebooted video editor focused on fast, streamlined edits with a PySide6 / Qt Quick frontend and a Python backend.

The core user flow is:

1. open a video
2. scrub precisely with a high-quality visual timeline
3. apply quick edits such as crop and segment removal
4. reload the current working video after each applied edit
5. export the final result only when desired

This project should strongly prefer simple architecture, thin vertical slices, TDD for backend and editing logic, and a deliberate test pyramid.

## Product and architecture goals

- Keep Qt Quick responsible for interaction and presentation.
- Keep Python responsible for editing logic and video operations.
- Hide FFmpeg behind small, stable backend services.
- Treat editing and export as separate concerns.
- Design undo/redo into the architecture from the start.
- Use `~/Code/videoeditor` as reference material only, never as the architectural template.

## Cross-cutting implementation rules

These rules apply to every editing slice below:

- Every editing feature must integrate with the chosen undo/redo approach.
- Applying an edit should refresh the current working video in the player.
- Business logic should be testable without Qt wherever possible.
- FFmpeg details should stay behind narrow service interfaces.
- UI-heavy features should still ship with enough tests to anchor behavior.

## Technical references, not hard dependencies

These are common concerns that many slices will touch. They should be solved early, but other tasks should reference them rather than depending rigidly on them.

- command and undo/redo model
- working video/session model
- backend media operation interfaces
- test fixture and video assertion helpers
- screenshot and headless GUI execution support

## Epics and thin vertical slices

## Epic 1: Foundations for fast, testable development

### 1. Write the project charter and agent guidance
Create the initial project docs that define principles, architecture expectations, and the rule that `~/Code/videoeditor` is reference-only.

Acceptance criteria:

- project principles are documented
- agent guidance clearly describes how to use the old project safely
- docs are concise enough to guide future implementation sessions

### 2. Establish the backend test harness with real video fixtures
Create the initial PyTest harness for backend work using real video fixtures and helper assertions.

Scope:

- fixture organization for source videos
- helper functions to probe metadata
- helper functions to sample frames
- helper functions to assert dimensions, duration bounds, and stream integrity
- a pattern for writing FFmpeg-backed integration tests

Acceptance criteria:

- PyTest is configured for fast unit and integration feedback
- at least one small fixture video is checked into the repo or reproducibly generated
- tests can verify crop dimensions and trim/cut duration changes
- the harness is simple to extend for later media operations

### 3. Establish GUI screenshot capture and headless test execution
Investigate and implement the baseline tooling for running GUI tests with screenshots and, where possible, headless execution.

Scope:

- QML test execution strategy
- screenshot capture for debugging failures
- CI-friendly headless execution approach if available
- documentation for local and automated usage

Acceptance criteria:

- at least one GUI test can capture a screenshot artifact
- a documented headless or low-disruption test path exists
- the approach is simple enough to use continuously during development

### 4. Choose and prove the undo/redo architecture
Define the undo/redo strategy that all editing features will follow.

Scope:

- evaluate Qt undo stack integration versus a backend-owned command stack exposed to QML
- model reversible edit commands
- define how command labels/history appear to the UI
- define how current working video state is restored or recomputed on undo/redo

Acceptance criteria:

- a documented architecture decision exists
- a minimal spike proves the chosen approach end to end
- tests demonstrate apply, undo, and redo for a simple edit command

## Epic 2: Walking skeleton editor loop

### 5. Build the minimal app shell with open, play, pause, seek, and reload
Create the smallest useful application that opens a video, displays it with Qt multimedia components, supports basic playback, and can reload a new working video path.

Scope:

- app bootstrap
- simple QML screen
- file open flow
- media player integration
- backend bridge for current working video path
- reload behavior after a backend-provided replacement video path

Acceptance criteria:

- a user can open a video and play, pause, and seek it
- the player can reload a replacement working video cleanly
- the backend/QML boundary is minimal and intentional
- tests cover the core non-visual logic and one happy-path UI interaction

### 6. Define the editor session model and media service boundaries
Create the initial backend model for the current editing session and the service interfaces that hide FFmpeg details.

Scope:

- source video versus current working video
- media probe service
- edit application service interfaces
- thumbnail service interface
- export service interface
- clear naming for edit requests and results

Acceptance criteria:

- the backend has explicit concepts for source and working video
- FFmpeg is not called directly from high-level application logic
- tests cover the session model and service contracts
- the design is small enough to support crop and cut slices without a god object

## Epic 3: Timeline foundation and signature scrubber

### 7. Ship a basic timeline scrubber bound to the player
Add a first useful timeline that reflects duration, current playhead position, and seeking.

Scope:

- timeline display in QML
- bidirectional binding between player position and scrubber
- precise seeking behavior
- basic visual playhead representation

Acceptance criteria:

- the playhead follows playback
- dragging or clicking the timeline seeks the video
- the feature is usable without frame thumbnails yet
- tests cover timeline math and one UI happy path

### 8. Add thumbnail extraction and thumbnail-backed timeline rendering
Upgrade the timeline so it renders extracted frame thumbnails across the scrubber.

Scope:

- thumbnail extraction service
- thumbnail caching strategy
- mapping thumbnails onto visible timeline width
- handling loading and failure states cleanly

Acceptance criteria:

- the timeline shows frame thumbnails for a loaded video
- thumbnails are cached or reused where appropriate
- the UI remains responsive during extraction
- tests cover backend extraction logic and focused QML rendering behavior

### 9. Polish the timeline into a high-quality visual scrubber
Refine the scrubber to feel much closer to iPhone Photos / iMovie style timeline interaction.

Scope:

- continuous visual layout of frames
- refined pointer interaction
- better responsiveness while scrubbing
- better visual affordances for precision
- performance tuning for long videos and repeated seeks

Acceptance criteria:

- scrubbing feels visually continuous and precise
- the timeline is clearly better than a generic slider
- performance is acceptable on representative fixture videos
- tests and profiling notes anchor the intended behavior

## Epic 4: Direct crop editing

### 10. Implement direct-manipulation crop selection on the video
Allow the user to drag directly on the video to create and adjust a crop rectangle without entering a separate crop mode.

Scope:

- direct drag-to-create crop rectangle
- inline resize and reposition handles
- clear visual affordances
- confirm/apply interaction
- command integration for undo/redo

Acceptance criteria:

- a user can create and adjust a crop rectangle directly on the video
- no separate crop mode is required
- the crop action is represented as an undoable command
- tests cover crop geometry and core UI behavior

### 11. Apply crop and reload the current working video
Connect the crop UI to the backend media service so applying a crop creates a new working video and reloads it.

Scope:

- crop request model
- backend crop service
- working video update flow
- error handling and progress reporting
- undo/redo integration for applied crops

Acceptance criteria:

- applying a crop produces a new working video
- the player reloads the cropped result automatically
- undo and redo restore the expected video state
- integration tests verify output dimensions and video validity

## Epic 5: Fast segment removal workflow

### 12. Add cut markers to the timeline
Allow users to place precise cut markers on the timeline with direct interaction.

Scope:

- marker creation from timeline interaction
- marker rendering and selection
- marker positioning rules
- command integration for add/remove/move marker actions

Acceptance criteria:

- a user can add multiple cut markers
- markers are clearly visible and adjustable
- marker operations are undoable and redoable
- tests cover marker ordering, constraints, and UI behavior

### 13. Add segment hover, highlight, and scissor affordances
Turn the space between cut markers into directly actionable segments that can be removed quickly.

Scope:

- segment hover states
- highlighted removable regions
- scissor affordance or equivalent action target
- direct remove-segment interaction

Acceptance criteria:

- hovering a segment clearly shows it can be removed
- removing a segment feels immediate and obvious
- the interaction matches the fast “mark mark mark, chop chop chop” workflow
- tests cover segment derivation and action triggering

### 14. Apply segment removal and reload the working video
Connect the timeline cut model to the backend so removed segments are rendered out and the result becomes the new working video.

Scope:

- segment removal request model
- backend cut/remove service
- concat strategy for keeping remaining segments
- working video refresh flow
- undo/redo integration

Acceptance criteria:

- a user can remove one or more middle segments from a video
- the edited result reloads as the current working video
- undo and redo restore the correct state
- integration tests verify duration changes and output validity

## Epic 6: Export the current result

### 15. Export the current working video as a separate save flow
Implement export as a distinct concern that saves the current working result to a user-chosen path.

Scope:

- save dialog flow
- export request model
- progress and error reporting
- cancellation if practical
- tests for export orchestration

Acceptance criteria:

- export operates on the current working video, not directly on edit tools
- a user can choose a destination and save the current result
- the export flow communicates progress and errors clearly
- tests cover the orchestration path and failure handling

## Epic 7: Confidence and polish

### 16. Add focused end-to-end happy-path tests
Create a small but valuable set of end-to-end tests that exercise the app through the UI and backend together.

Candidate flows:

- open video -> crop -> apply -> export
- open video -> add cut markers -> remove segment -> export
- open video -> perform edit -> undo -> redo

Acceptance criteria:

- at least two end-to-end flows exist
- failures produce useful screenshots or logs
- the suite remains intentionally small and maintainable

### 17. Improve keyboard and precision interactions
Refine the editor with a small set of high-value precision interactions.

Candidate scope:

- keyboard nudging for crop or markers
- keyboard shortcuts for add cut, undo, redo, export
- snapping or precision aids for marker placement

Acceptance criteria:

- the chosen interactions improve speed without adding complexity
- the refinements work with the existing undo/redo model
- tests cover the most important behaviors

### 18. Tune performance and maintainability
Review hotspots in thumbnail extraction, video reloading, and timeline responsiveness, and simplify the code where needed.

Scope:

- identify the slowest workflows
- measure enough to guide improvements
- simplify APIs or components that drift toward unnecessary complexity
- record follow-up work for larger improvements

Acceptance criteria:

- major interaction bottlenecks have been investigated
- the implementation remains aligned with the project principles
- identified follow-up work is captured cleanly

## Slice ordering recommendation

Recommended first implementation order:

1. foundations docs
2. backend test harness
3. GUI screenshot/headless testing baseline
4. undo/redo architecture decision and spike
5. minimal app shell with open/play/reload
6. editor session model and service boundaries
7. basic scrubber
8. thumbnail timeline
9. direct crop selection
10. crop apply/reload
11. cut markers
12. removable segments
13. segment removal apply/reload
14. export flow
15. end-to-end tests and polish

This order keeps the project usable early while protecting the architecture from a late undo/redo refactor.

name: Crop
description:
First editing feature: select a crop region, apply it, and support undo/redo.

This is the first vertical slice that modifies the working video. It proves the full editing loop:
- Direct manipulation crop selection on the video surface
- Apply crop through backend media service
- Reload the cropped result as the new working video
- Undo/redo restores previous video state

The undo/redo architecture emerges here — designed for a real feature, not in isolation.

Acceptance criteria:
- User can drag on the video to create/adjust a crop rectangle
- Applying crop produces a new working video and reloads the player
- Undo reverts to the previous video state
- Redo reapplies the crop
- No separate crop mode required (direct manipulation)
- Tests cover crop geometry, backend service, and undo/redo behavior

name: Establish GUI screenshot capture and headless test execution
description:
Establish the baseline GUI testing workflow for Qt Quick / QML, including screenshot capture and a headless or low-disruption execution path.

Context:
- The project wants strong visual debugging support from the start.
- GUI tests should exist, but they should stay focused and not replace faster lower-level tests.
- When UI tests fail, screenshot artifacts should make failures easier to understand.

Scope:
- Investigate the best approach for QML/Qt UI testing in this repo.
- Implement at least one example GUI test that can produce a screenshot artifact.
- Investigate and document a headless or low-disruption way to run GUI tests locally and in automation if feasible.
- Document how to run these tests and where artifacts are stored.

Cross-cutting notes:
- This work supports later timeline, crop, and end-to-end slices.
- Keep the setup simple enough for continuous use, not just one-off debugging.
- The goal is confidence and debuggability, not a giant brittle UI suite.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least one GUI test can capture a screenshot artifact.
- A documented headless or low-disruption execution path exists if the stack supports it.
- The workflow is practical enough to use during normal development.
- The resulting approach is documented for future sessions.
result:
Established GUI test infrastructure with screenshot capture and headless support.

Fixtures:
- app_window: Creates full QML app window via QQmlApplicationEngine
- capture_screenshot: Uses QScreen.grabWindow() to capture PNGs

Tests:
- 3 GUI tests validating window title, visibility, size
- Screenshot captured to tests/screenshots/ (gitignored)

Headless execution:
- QT_QPA_PLATFORM=offscreen works fully
- make test-headless runs all tests without display

Makefile targets:
- make test-gui: Run GUI tests with window
- make test-headless: Run all tests headless

Verification:
- 32 total tests passing
- Screenshot capture verified (initial_state.png ~1.2MB)
- Headless mode verified

Dependencies: pytest-qt added

commit.message: Add GUI test infrastructure with screenshot capture

name: Polish the timeline into a high-quality visual scrubber
description:
Polish the timeline into a high-quality visual scrubber closer to iPhone Photos / iMovie style interaction.

Context:
- The scrubber is a signature feature and deserves dedicated refinement.
- The goal is a visually continuous, precise, responsive experience rather than a generic desktop slider.

Scope:
- Improve the continuity of frame layout.
- Refine pointer interaction and scrubbing feel.
- Improve precision affordances.
- Tune responsiveness for long videos and repeated seeks.
- Measure enough performance to make informed improvements.

Cross-cutting notes:
- This is a refinement slice on top of the earlier scrubber and thumbnail slices.
- Keep the implementation simpler than the UX is polished.
- Record any deeper performance or architecture follow-ups cleanly in dex.

Acceptance criteria:
- Scrubbing feels visually continuous and precise.
- The timeline is clearly better than a generic slider.
- Performance is acceptable on representative fixture videos.
- Tests and/or profiling notes anchor the intended behavior.

name: Export the current working video as a separate save flow
description:
Implement export as a separate save flow that operates on the current working video.

Context:
- Export is distinct from editing.
- By the time export runs, the user already has a current working result that may include crop, segment removal, or other edits.

Scope:
- Implement destination selection.
- Define the export request/orchestration model.
- Communicate progress and errors clearly.
- Add cancellation if practical without undue complexity.
- Cover export orchestration with tests.

Cross-cutting notes:
- Export should not be coupled to a specific edit tool.
- The feature should operate on the current working video abstraction.
- Keep the workflow understandable from both QML and backend perspectives.

Acceptance criteria:
- Export operates on the current working video, not directly on edit tools.
- A user can choose a destination and save the current result.
- The flow communicates progress and errors clearly.
- Tests cover orchestration and failure handling.

name: Add thumbnail extraction and thumbnail-backed timeline rendering
description:
Add thumbnail extraction and thumbnail-backed rendering to the timeline.

Context:
- The visual timeline is central to the intended UX.
- Users should be able to read the video visually from the scrubber, not just from a generic slider.

Scope:
- Implement thumbnail extraction service behavior.
- Add a caching strategy suitable for repeated scrubbing and reloads.
- Map extracted frames onto the visible timeline width in QML.
- Handle loading, empty, and failure states cleanly.

Cross-cutting notes:
- Keep extraction off the critical UI path so the interface remains responsive.
- The service design should fit the backend media boundaries established earlier.
- This slice prepares for the later high-polish scrubber and cut-segment UI.

Acceptance criteria:
- The timeline shows representative frame thumbnails for a loaded video.
- Thumbnails are cached or reused where appropriate.
- The UI remains responsive during extraction.
- Tests cover extraction logic and focused QML rendering behavior.

name: Write the project charter and agent guidance
description:
Create the initial project-facing guidance for Video Scissors. This task establishes the written rules that future implementation work should follow.

Context:
- This repository is a reboot of ~/Code/videoeditor, not a continuation of its architecture.
- Qt Quick / QML owns interaction and presentation.
- Python owns editing logic and media operations.
- FFmpeg must remain behind narrow backend service interfaces.
- Editing and export are separate concerns.
- Undo/redo is foundational and must not be deferred as polish.

Deliverables:
- Document the project principles.
- Document the intended architectural direction in concise, practical terms.
- Document that ~/Code/videoeditor is reference-only and must never be treated as the architectural template.
- Give future agents enough guidance to avoid drifting toward a god-object backend or mode-heavy UX.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project principles are documented in the repo.
- Guidance clearly explains how to use the old project safely as implementation mining only.
- The docs are concise, actionable, and suitable for future handoff sessions.
- Dex remains the source of truth; if markdown planning docs are used during planning, their content must be reflected back into dex tasks.
result:
Created project charter and agent guidance documents:

- docs/project-principles.md: 15 principles covering architecture, testing, UX direction, and workflow
- docs/architecture.md: Key architectural rules including editing/export separation, undo/redo as foundational, FFmpeg behind service boundaries
- docs/vision.md: Product and UX direction
- AGENTS.md: Lean operational guidance pointing to other docs and dex

All docs consistently document that ~/Code/videoeditor is reference material only. Dex is established as the source of truth for planning (plan.md was created then deleted, content moved to dex tasks).

name: Walking skeleton
description:
Get the first end-to-end slice running: test infrastructure, session model, and a minimal playable app shell.

This sub-epic groups the foundational work needed before feature slices (crop, cut, export) can begin. The goal is a working app that can open, play, and reload a video — proving the QML/Python boundary and backend structure work together.

Child tasks:
- Backend test harness with real video fixtures
- GUI screenshot capture and headless test execution
- Editor session model and media service boundaries
- Minimal app shell with open, play, pause, seek, and reload
result:
Walking skeleton complete. All four foundational tasks done:

1. Backend test harness (zmt0nadl):
   - PyTest + uv setup
   - FFmpeg testsrc fixture generation
   - Probe helpers for dimensions/duration

2. Session model & services (dhzr94mr):
   - EditorSession: source_video/working_video tracking
   - Service protocols: MediaProbe, Edit, Thumbnail, Export
   - Frozen dataclasses for requests/results

3. App shell (avb94jw9):
   - PySide6/QML app with video playback
   - SessionBridge: narrow Python↔QML boundary
   - Open/play/pause/seek/close functionality

4. GUI testing (k0inith2):
   - pytest-qt integration
   - Screenshot capture to tests/screenshots/
   - Headless mode via QT_QPA_PLATFORM=offscreen

Summary:
- 32 tests passing
- App runnable: make run
- Tests runnable: make test / make test-headless
- Clean architecture ready for crop/cut/export slices

name: Cut
description:
Second editing feature: mark segments for removal, visualize them, and apply the cut.

Vertical slice for segment removal:
- Add cut markers directly on the timeline
- Segment hover, highlight, and scissor affordances
- Apply segment removal through backend media service
- Reload the cut result as the new working video
- Undo/redo support (following the pattern established in Crop)

Acceptance criteria:
- User can place cut markers on the timeline
- Segments between markers are visually distinct with clear affordances
- User can remove a segment directly from the timeline
- Applying removal produces a new working video and reloads the player
- Undo/redo works for cut operations
- Tests cover marker placement, segment logic, and backend service

name: Establish the backend test harness with real video fixtures
description:
Establish the backend testing harness for media operations using PyTest and real video fixtures.

Context:
- Backend and core editing logic should be developed TDD-first whenever practical.
- The harness should support fast iteration while still giving confidence in FFmpeg-backed behavior.
- The project needs a repeatable pattern for validating operations like crop and segment removal without depending on the UI layer.

Scope:
- Configure PyTest for the new repository.
- Organize source video fixtures or define a reproducible fixture-generation approach.
- Add helper utilities to probe metadata, inspect stream integrity, sample frames, and assert expected dimensions and duration bounds.
- Establish a simple pattern for FFmpeg-backed integration tests.
- Keep helpers small and intention-revealing.

Cross-cutting notes:
- Hide FFmpeg specifics behind helper APIs where that improves test readability.
- Keep the pyramid deliberate: most detailed behavioral tests should remain below the UI layer.
- The harness should be reusable by crop, cut/remove, thumbnail, and export orchestration work.

Acceptance criteria:
- PyTest is configured and runnable in the new repo.
- At least one small representative fixture video exists, checked in or reproducibly generated.
- Tests can verify crop output dimensions.
- Tests can verify trim/cut style duration changes.
- Tests can assert that resulting media still has valid streams and readable frames.
- The harness is simple to extend for later media operations.
result:
Established minimal backend test harness with uv + pytest.

Structure:
- pyproject.toml with modern uv setup (dependency-groups for dev deps)
- tests/conftest.py with fixture generation and probe helpers
- tests/test_media_fixtures.py validating the approach

Key capabilities:
- generate_test_video(): Creates videos via FFmpeg testsrc (no external fixtures needed)
- probe_video(), get_video_stream(): FFprobe wrapper for metadata
- get_dimensions(), get_duration(): Assertion helpers

Verification:
- 5 tests passing covering dimension, duration, and stream assertions
- Tests run via `uv run pytest` (auto-syncs deps)
- Custom dimension/duration generation validated

This is intentionally minimal - establishes the pattern without over-building. Crop/trim/export tests can extend these helpers as needed.

commit.message: Set up backend test harness with pytest and video fixtures
